personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Justin is a Hufflepuff Muggle-born who is friendly, somewhat naive, and socially eager. He is notably trusting and open — he tells Harry he was destined for Eton before Hogwarts, signaling a certain middle-class pride. He becomes suspicious and fearful of Harry during CoS, showing he can shift from warmth to hostility under pressure. He is not a comic character but a realistic peer with social texture.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The corpus provided is empty. Evaluation relies on general knowledge: Justin appears very briefly in CoS with no meaningful dialogue or characterization. His personality is not established.
personality_voice.difference: The film gives Justin no space to establish any personality traits — his friendliness, his Muggle-born pride, his fear of Harry, or his social texture are all absent.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: With no corpus evidence and near-zero film characterization, a score in the lower range is appropriate. The character is present in name only.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In CoS, Justin serves a specific narrative function: he is one of Harry's Muggle-born peers who becomes a victim of the Basilisk and, crucially, one of the characters who suspects Harry of being the Heir of Slytherin. This gives him agency as a social antagonist and as a plot-relevant victim. His petrification raises stakes and his prior suspicion of Harry adds dramatic irony.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: Justin appears in CoS but his role as a suspicious peer is not developed. His petrification occurs but without the prior social tension that gives it narrative weight.
narrative_role_agency.difference: His function as a suspicious Muggle-born peer who actively distrusts Harry — a key narrative beat — is removed. He is reduced to a background victim.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: Loss of his active narrative role as a suspicious peer and his agency in confronting or avoiding Harry warrants a penalty. His petrification loses dramatic context.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Justin's motivations are clear in CoS: fear of the Heir of Slytherin, distrust of Harry based on circumstantial evidence, and self-preservation as a Muggle-born. There is a small but real internal conflict between his earlier friendliness toward Harry and his later fear.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: No motivation is established in the films. His shift from friendliness to suspicion is not shown.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The internal conflict between trust and fear is entirely absent.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: Motivations are absent rather than simplified, warranting a score in the lower range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Justin has a minor but complete arc in CoS: initial friendliness toward Harry, growing suspicion, petrification, and implicit resolution when the truth is revealed. It is a small arc but structurally present.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The arc is not present in the films. Only the petrification beat survives, without the before or after.
character_arc.difference: The arc's beginning (friendliness, then suspicion) and resolution are removed.
character_arc.penalty_logic: An arc that exists in the books but is reduced to a single plot point in the films warrants a penalty, though the arc was always minor.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Justin's most important relationship in the books is with Harry — moving from friendly acquaintance to fearful suspect. There is also a brief dynamic with Ernie Macmillan, who shares his suspicions. These relationships define his CoS characterization.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: Neither relationship is developed in the films. Justin and Harry have no meaningful interaction that establishes the shift in dynamic.
key_relationships.difference: The Harry-Justin dynamic, which is the core of his book characterization, is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: Loss of the defining relationship warrants a penalty.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Justin's complexity comes from the contrast between his open, friendly Muggle-born identity and his capacity for fear-driven suspicion. His Eton detail adds social texture. His willingness to publicly distrust Harry adds a layer of peer pressure and social dynamics rarely shown in the series.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: None of this texture is present in the films.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: All nuance is absent. The character is a background extra.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The character is reduced to near-zero complexity in the films.

lost_or_transferred_material: Justin's Muggle-born pride and Eton background detail
Justin's active suspicion of Harry as the Heir of Slytherin
The social dynamic between Justin and Ernie Macmillan as suspicious peers
Justin's arc from friendliness to fear and back
The dramatic irony of Harry being suspected by a character he had befriended
score_caps_applied: Cap 4 applied: films preserve only broad identity (Hufflepuff student, petrification victim) while removing most of what defines the book characterization
comparative.key_observations: Both the book and film corpus provided are empty, so this evaluation relies entirely on general knowledge of the series. Justin Finch-Fletchley is a minor character whose entire book relevance is concentrated in CoS, where he functions as a suspicious Muggle-born peer and Basilisk victim. The films reduce him to a background presence with no dialogue, no established personality, no developed relationships, and no arc. The score reflects near-total loss of characterization rather than faithful compression. Confidence is Low across all dimensions due to the absence of corpus material.
